settings.fallback_subtitle: A clear one-line description of what this guide covers and who it's for.
settings.complete_desc: You now have what you need to put this into practice. The kit below makes it stupid-simple to start.
settings.cta_title: Get the kit and start your first batch this weekend.
settings.cta_desc: Pre-measured spice mixes, the right jar, an airlock, and a recipe book that includes everything from this guide.

settings.title: Why fermentation?
settings.intro: Quick orientation: what fermentation is, why it's been a kitchen staple for thousands of years, and what you're actually going to do over the next 30 days.
settings.lesson:
Fermentation is one of the oldest food-preservation techniques humans have. Salt, water, vegetables, and time — that's the recipe. The bacteria already living on the food do the work.

Why it matters now: live cultures, easy on the gut, vastly cheaper than store-bought probiotics, and connects you to a kitchen craft your grandparents probably used.
settings.takeaways:
Fermentation = controlled bacterial preservation
Lactic acid bacteria do the work, not heat or chemicals
Traditional in every food culture: kimchi, sauerkraut, kanji, dosa
Live cultures are the active ingredient
settings.action_text: Pick the ferment you want to make first. Most beginners go with cabbage (sauerkraut) or carrots — both are very forgiving.

settings.title: Set up your kit
settings.intro: What you need physically. The kit covers everything; this section shows you what each piece does and how to clean it.
settings.lesson:
Your kit ships with a fermentation jar, an airlock lid, a glass weight, and pre-measured spice mixes. Wash everything once with hot soapy water and air dry — no need for sterilisation chemicals.

The airlock lets CO₂ out (so the jar doesn't explode) but keeps oxygen out (so mould can't grow). It's the single most important piece.
settings.takeaways:
Wash with soap + hot water (no sterilisation needed)
Airlock = CO₂ out, oxygen blocked
Glass weight keeps vegetables submerged
Kitchen counter at 22–25°C is the right environment
settings.action_text: Wash your kit and set it on a counter where it can sit undisturbed for 5–7 days. Out of direct sunlight.

settings.title: Your first batch
settings.intro: Start with the simplest possible ferment: shredded cabbage with salt. This walks you through it.
settings.lesson:
Shred 1kg cabbage. Add 20g salt (2%). Massage by hand for 5 minutes — your goal is wet cabbage with brine forming. Pack into the jar, push down so the brine covers everything, drop in the glass weight, seal with the airlock.

Set on the counter. Wait 5–7 days. Taste from day 5; refrigerate when you like the flavour.
settings.takeaways:
1kg cabbage → 20g salt (2% by weight)
Massage 5 min until brine forms
Keep vegetables submerged
Taste daily from day 5; refrigerate when ready
settings.action_text: Make the batch today. Note the date. Set a reminder for day 5 to check.

settings.title: Read the signs
settings.intro: What's normal vs what's gone wrong. The single most important troubleshooting skill is knowing what success looks like.
settings.lesson:
Day 1–2: quiet. A few bubbles, brine cloudy, smells like raw cabbage.

Day 3–5: active. Clear bubbles rising, brine cloudy, smells tangy and slightly funky.

Day 5–7: settling. Bubble production slows, smell mellows.

Bad signs: fuzzy mould (any colour), sliminess, strong rotten smell. Throw it out — don't try to save a bad batch.
settings.takeaways:
Cloudy brine + bubbles = healthy ferment
Tangy-funky smell = good
Fuzzy mould = bad, throw out
White scum on surface (kahm yeast) = harmless, scrape off
settings.action_text: Check your batch. Take a photo each day so you can see the progression.

settings.title: Daily routine
settings.intro: Once you have your first batch in the fridge, here's the routine that keeps fermentation in your life forever.
settings.lesson:
Eat 1–2 tablespoons a day with meals. Once you finish a batch, start the next one the same day — that way you always have one fermenting and one in the fridge.

Vary the vegetables: cabbage, carrots, beetroot, kohlrabi, radishes. Add spices to taste. Swap salts (sea salt vs Himalayan vs kosher) and notice the flavour change.
settings.takeaways:
1–2 tbsp a day with meals
Keep one batch fermenting + one in fridge
Vary vegetables every batch
Document your favourites
settings.action_text: Start your second batch with a different vegetable. Note what you change.

settings.title: Going further
settings.intro: You've made a successful batch. Here's where to go next: more complex ferments, dairy ferments, sourdough, fermented drinks.
settings.lesson:
Vegetable ferments: kimchi, achaar, beetroot kvass, hot sauce.

Dairy ferments: yogurt, kefir, paneer-style cheese.

Drink ferments: water kefir, kombucha, kanji, jun.

Sourdough: wild-yeast bread that takes the principles you've learned and applies them to grain.

Pick one. Don't try to do all of these at once. Each is its own learning curve.
settings.takeaways:
Master one ferment before adding another
Different ferments use different cultures
Specific kits exist for each (kimchi, kefir, kanji, sourdough)
Pace yourself — fermentation is a craft, not a sprint
settings.action_text: Pick the ferment you most want to learn next. Order the kit. Stop reading; start making.
